summary: Gruber writes a satirical fictional diary from the perspective of an App Store reviewer, exposing the arbitrary, capricious, and Kafkaesque nature of Apple's app review process. The reviewer deliberately rejects apps inconsistently, finding personal satisfaction in wielding unchecked power over developers. Through dark humor, Gruber draws a chilling parallel between the reviewer's discretionary authority and the East German Stasi's methods of psychological control. The piece highlights how developers face an opaque, impersonal system where identical violations are enforced selectively, and appeals are met with robotic form responses. The final punchline — rejecting an app both for resembling an iPhone and for not resembling one — crystallizes the absurdity of the entire process.
key_points: App Store rejections are applied inconsistently, with identical violations tolerated in some apps but used to reject others
The extended Stasi analogy suggests Apple's review system operates through psychological dominance — developers are broken not by the rules but by the unknowability of the system
The reviewer's internal monologue reveals that capriciousness is the point: 'To act fairly would be to follow the rules. To act capriciously is to be the rules'
Developers are trapped in an asymmetric relationship where they hold no cards and cannot even confirm a human is reading their appeals
The catch-22 ending — rejected for looking like an iPhone, then rejected for not looking like one — encapsulates the system's fundamental absurdity
money_quotes: To act fairly would be to follow the rules. To act capriciously is to be the rules.
The feeling of being part of the monolith — of being the monolith — really only surges when I use my position to act capriciously.
I could read that over and over. It's like the voice of a robot. The voice of authority. The voice of authoritative robots.

He is hoping there is someone like me reading his plea, an individual who can see the injustice and absurdity of the situation and who could, with the click of a button, make it right. And in fact his hope is correct.

It is a very important relationship in his life and it is not going well, and in this particular very important relationship he holds none of the cards and the entity on the other side holds them all, but does not care, and worse, does not even notice.
What drove him mad wasn't that he thought he was still being followed, but that he realized that even if he wasn't still being followed, he would never know. It would never end.

I did this deliberately, knowing that if there were two well-known apps available in the Store which violate this dubious interpretation of Apple's copyright guidelines, it would be far more satisfying to reject only the third such app.
key_insight: Apple's App Store review process is not merely flawed but structurally designed to be opaque and arbitrary, giving individual reviewers unchecked discretionary power that mirrors authoritarian systems of psychological control.
